--

Why HTTP Finally Got a QUERY Method: Solving a 25-Year REST Problem
For years, developers have relied on a familiar set of HTTP methods — GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, and DELETE. These methods have become second nature to anyone building APIs.
Then, in 2026, HTTP introduced something new: QUERY.
At first glance, it might seem like just another method added to the protocol. But the real story isn’t about a new keyword. It’s about solving an architectural problem that developers have been working around for years.
Let’s understand why this matters.
It All Starts with TCP
Before HTTP existed, computers communicated over transport protocols like TCP.
TCP is excellent at reliably moving bytes from one machine to another, but it doesn’t understand what those bytes mean.
Imagine sending this over TCP:
HelloOr even:
1The receiver gets the data successfully, but has no idea what the sender intended.

• Is it requesting data?

• Creating a user?

• Updating a record?

• Deleting something?
TCP only guarantees delivery — not meaning.

HTTP Introduced Intent
HTTP solved this by standardizing communication.
Instead of sending arbitrary text, every request carries an explicit intent.
Examples include:
GET /usersPOST /usersDELETE /users/1Now the server immediately understands what the client wants.
An HTTP request became more than raw data — it became structured communication consisting of:

• HTTP Method (intent)

• Resource

• Headers

• Optional Body
This standardization made it possible for browsers, servers, proxies, APIs, and frameworks to speak the same language.
Why GET Became So Powerful
GET requests have one extremely important property:
They are idempotent.
That means calling the same endpoint repeatedly should not change server state.
For example:
GET /users/1Whether executed once or a thousand times, the response should remain the same (assuming the underlying data hasn’t changed).
Because of this property, GET requests can safely be cached.
This is where CDNs become incredibly valuable.
Instead of every request reaching your backend and database:
Client
↓
CDN Cache
↓
Server
↓
DatabaseThe CDN can return cached responses immediately, reducing:

• Server load

• Database queries

• Network latency

• Infrastructure costs
This is one of the biggest reasons modern web applications scale efficiently.
The Problem with Real-World Searches
Simple filters fit nicely into URLs:
GET /users?name=JohnBut production APIs rarely stay simple.
A realistic search might require:

• Multiple filters

• Sorting

• Pagination

• Nested conditions

• Date ranges

• Arrays

• Complex logical expressions
Very quickly, URLs become enormous.
Example:
GET /users?
name=John&
gender=male&
age<30&
department=engineering&
emailContains=gmail&
...This creates several problems:

• URL length limitations

• Difficult encoding

• Poor readability

• Sensitive filters appearing in logs

• Hard-to-maintain APIs
Eventually, GET starts feeling like the wrong tool.
The Workaround Everyone Used
Developers found an easy solution.
Instead of GET, they used POST.
POST /searchUsersRequest body:
{
"name": "John",
"age": {
"lt": 30
},
"department": "Engineering",
"email": {
"contains": "gmail.com"
}
}Now developers could send arbitrarily complex JSON.
Problem solved?
Not exactly.
Why POST Was Never the Right Answer
POST is designed for creating resources.
It is not considered idempotent.
Calling:
POST /usersfive times might create five different users.
Because POST isn’t guaranteed to be idempotent:

• CDNs don’t cache it by default.

• Infrastructure treats it as a state-changing request.

• Many optimizations disappear.
Ironically, developers were using POST only because they needed a request body — not because they wanted to create data.
It worked, but it violated REST semantics.
Enter HTTP QUERY
The new QUERY method fixes this gap.
Conceptually, it says:
“I am performing a read-only operation, but I need a request body.”
Instead of writing:
POST /searchUsersYou can write:
QUERY /userswith a JSON body:
{
"filters": {
"name": "John",
"age": {
"lt": 30
},
"department": "Engineering"
}
}Now the intent is perfectly clear.
The request is:

• Read-only

• Idempotent

• Cacheable

• Able to carry complex request bodies
Why This Matters for System Design
From a system-design perspective, QUERY enables:
Better CDN Caching
Since QUERY represents a safe read operation, intermediaries can cache responses more intelligently.
Lower Backend Load
Repeated complex searches no longer have to hit the database every time.
Cleaner APIs
Developers no longer need awkward endpoints like:
POST /searchUsersor
POST /getReportsThe endpoint remains resource-oriented while the HTTP method accurately describes the operation.
Better Protocol Semantics
Instead of bending POST into something it was never designed for, the protocol now officially supports this use case.
Will QUERY Replace GET?
No.
GET remains the best choice for:

• Simple retrieval

• Short query parameters

• Browser navigation

• Bookmarkable URLs
QUERY is intended for situations where the request becomes too complex for a URL but should still be treated as a safe, cacheable read.
Think of it as a specialized tool — not a replacement.
Final Thoughts
The QUERY method doesn’t introduce a new capability that developers couldn’t already achieve.
What it introduces is standardization.
For years, teams relied on POST-based workarounds for complex searches, sacrificing REST semantics and caching benefits in the process.
QUERY formalizes a pattern the industry has already been using while preserving the advantages of safe, idempotent, cache-friendly requests.
Sometimes the biggest protocol improvements aren’t about doing something new — they’re about giving the right name and behavior to something developers have needed all along.
